coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/cobalt chalcogenide porous nanofibers for highly efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bin
Sun, Changchun
Wang, Chao
Jiang, Zhan
Han, Guangting
Zhao, Haiguang
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen is increasingly needed as a clean energy source with high energy density. Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) via electrocatalysis is a viable way to produce clean hydrogen. The key to achieving active HER is to select highly-active electrocatalysts. Although Pt-based noble metal catalysts have excellent performance, they are expensive and earth-abundant. Compared to noble metals, cobalt chalcogenide has been used for efficient HER. Here, we first demonstrated a porous material based on C-dots/cobalt chalcogenide nanofibers as an effective electrocatalyst for HER. Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers-based electrode reached an overpotential of 383 mV at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2 with Tafel slope of 93.4 mV·dec−1 in 1 M KOH, which is much better than bare Co3O4 (758 mV at 10 mA·cm−2, Tafel slope is 111.3 mV·dec−1). In addition to the excellent HER performance, the Co3O4/C-dots nanofibers performed very well in terms of stability after a 10-hour operation, and the overpotential was even reduced by 6.7%. With the further sulphuration, the Co3S4/C-dots nanofibers exhibited an overpotential of 280.2 mV at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2. This work indicates that the C-dots can be used as an efficient additive for cobalt chalcogenide based electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt chalcogenide nanofibers
Carbon quantum dots
Electrospinning
Electrocatalytic
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H. Zhao acknowledges the funding support from Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial Education Department.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images and size distribution of the (a, b) Co3O4 porous nanofibers and (c, d) Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers. Insets are the high-magnification SEM images of the nanofibers.

(a) FTIR spectra of Co3O4 porous nanofibers, Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers and C-dots. (b) TEM image of Co3O4 porous nanofibers and the inset is HRTEM image. (c) HRTEM image of C-dots. (d) TEM image of Co3O4 /10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers and the inset is HRTEM image. (e) TEM image of Co3S4 /10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers and the inset is HRTEM image. (f) SAED pattern of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers.

(a) XRD patterns of C-dots and Co3O4, Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots, and Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers. (b) XPS full spectra of Co3O4 porous nanofibers and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers. (c-d) The high-resolution C 1 s and Co 2p XPS spectra of Co3O4 porous nanofibers and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers.

(a) HER polarization curves, (b) Tafel slopes, (c) double layer current difference vs. scan rates plots, and (d) EIS, the inset is fitted equivalent circuit, (e) the histogram drawn of the values of Cdl of carbon paper, Co3O4, Co3O4/1 wt% C-dots, Co3O4/5 wt% C-dots, Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots and Co3O4/15 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers. (f) Polarization curves of the Co3O4 and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers before and after stability test, and inset is chronopotentiometry of the Co3O4 and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers at a constant current density of 10 mA·cm−2.
(a) HER polarization curves, (b) Tafel slopes, (c) double layer current difference vs. scan rates plots, and (d) EIS, the inset is fitted equivalent circuit, of Co3S4, Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers.
Summary of HER performance of cobalt-based catalysts in 1 M KOH.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/cobalt chalcogenide porous nanofibers for highly efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Material Engineering, Yantai University, No. 30 Qingquan Road, Yantai 264005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Material Engineering, Yantai University, No. 30 Qingquan Road, Yantai 264005, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen is increasingly needed as a clean energy source with high energy density. Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) via electrocatalysis is a viable way to produce clean hydrogen. The key to achieving active HER is to select highly-active electrocatalysts. Although Pt-based noble metal catalysts have excellent performance, they are expensive and earth-abundant. Compared to noble metals, cobalt chalcogenide has been used for efficient HER. Here, we first demonstrated a porous material based on C-dots/cobalt chalcogenide nanofibers as an effective electrocatalyst for HER. Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers-based electrode reached an overpotential of 383 mV at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2 with Tafel slope of 93.4 mV·dec−1 in 1 M KOH, which is much better than bare Co3O4 (758 mV at 10 mA·cm−2, Tafel slope is 111.3 mV·dec−1). In addition to the excellent HER performance, the Co3O4/C-dots nanofibers performed very well in terms of stability after a 10-hour operation, and the overpotential was even reduced by 6.7%. With the further sulphuration, the Co3S4/C-dots nanofibers exhibited an overpotential of 280.2 mV at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2. This work indicates that the C-dots can be used as an efficient additive for cobalt chalcogenide based electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen energy has attracted great attention due to its high energy power density and clean byproduct after combustion. It has represented a promising alternative to fossil fuels, which have led to climate change and environmental pollution [1]. Many approaches have been used for hydrogen production including fossil fuel hydrogen production, microbial hydrogen production, and electrolysis of water hydrogen production [2,3]. Among these approaches, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) via electrocatalysis is a potentially viable way to produce clean hydrogen [4]. The key factor to affect the electricity-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency is the functional performance of the electrocatalysts. To date, noble metals (e.g., Pt) are the most effective electrocatalysts, while the cost of these metals is very high and the abundance of these metals is limited [5–8]. It is very important to select other cost-effective earth-abundant electrocatalysts to replace noble metals for HER.

Transition metals were used as effective electrocatalysts for HER owing to their high HER performance [9–15]. Among various types of transition metals, Co-based materials are suitable for the electrocatalytic HER with promising activity and stability [16–18]. For instance, Co3O4 and Co3S4 has been widely used as a catalytic material for HER [19–25]. Benefiting from its excellent HER reaction kinetics, which can be detected from its low Tafel slope, the HER performance of the Co-based electrodes is significantly improved compared to other types of transition metal composites. Sun et al. reported the electrocatalysts prepared by loading MoS2 onto Co3O4 nanosheet arrays, which have more active sites and faster electronic charge transfer performance compared to single material [20]. Jiang et al. reported an efficient HER catalyst material obtained by loading single-phase Co3O4 on the surface of N-doped carbon nanotubes [22]. Sun et al. reported a catalyst material composed of CoSe2, Co3S4 and Co3O4 with high HER performance [24]. Although Co-based materials have shown great potential for HER, the obtained HER performance is still below that of the Pt-based electrode and there is still a big room to enhance the efficiency of the electrochemical cells. To date, it is still a challenge to produce cobalt chalcogenide-based electrocatalysts with high current density at low overpotential, which may be largely affected by their limited surface area, and low electron and mass transfer performance [17,26–30].

Carbon materials (e.g., carbon nanotube or graphene) have been used as conducting materials to improve the electron and mass transfer performance [31–37]. While these materials tend to aggregate during the preparation of the hybrid materials, which limits their performance of the HER [38–40]. Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have very small size and surface functional groups, making them easily dispersed in aqueous solution [41]. Currently, C-dots have been used as additives in electrocatalysts for HER owing to their excellent conductivity, rapid electron transfer, and electronic storage properties [42]. For example, the graphene-like structure could improve the charge transfer inside the cobalt oxides [43]. There is still lack of investigation of the C-dots effect on the performance of the cobalt chalcogenide materials, especially for the Co3O4 or Co3S4. The adding of the C-dots could improve the electron transfer rate and active area of the catalysts. Electrostatic spinning is an excellent technique for preparing polymer fibers, allowing simple preparation of hollow or porous nanofibers [44–46]. The hollow and porous structural features can effectively increase the electrocatalytic activity area of the catalyst. While the conductivity of the nanofibers is still not high, which limits their potential use in the HER. Based on the above considerations, we aimed to combine the advantages of C-dots and electrospinning technology to rapidly prepare porous nanofibers with high electrical conductivity and good HRE performance.

In this work, we demonstrated a porous nanostructure based on C-dots and cobalt chalcogenide nanofibers for the effective generation of H2 under alkaline condition. The hybrid nanofibers were prepared via electrospinning using cobalt metal salts and C-dots as spinning solution and further sintered at 310 ℃. The presence of the C-dots can not only decrease the size of the nanofibers, but also improve the charge transfer between the oxide and C-dots. At the same time, the porous hybrid nanofibers can effectively prevent C-dots from escaping into the electrolyte while providing a larger reaction area. Benefiting from these advantages, the as-prepared C-dots/ cobalt chalcogenide nanofiber -based catalysts achieved an improved performance in 1 M KOH solution compared to that of the Co3O4 porous nanofibers.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA), urea, anhydrous calcium chloride, CH2Cl2, and methanol were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, MW = 150000) was purchased from Macklin. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 1300000) and sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O) were purchased from Aladdin. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate (C4H6CoO4·4H2O) were purchased from Hushi. Nafion 117 solution was purchased from Aldrich, and epoxy resin ab adhesive model V08 was purchased from Setllom. All materials were directly used as received without further purification.

A space-confined vacuum heating approach was used to produce C-dots [47]. In detail, a mixture containing 1 g of CA, 2 g of urea, and 1 g of CaCl2 was dissolved in 5 mL of water. After completely dissolving the powder, the clear mixture was transferred into a flask and heated to 120 °C under a vacuum. After 30 min, the temperature was raised to 180 °C and maintained at this temperature for 30 min. Finally, the reaction temperature was raised to 250 °C and maintained at this temperature for 2 h. After cooling, the powder was collected and further dispersed in methanol. The chromatographic silica column was used to purify the C-dots using a mixture of methanol and CH2Cl2 (methanol: CH2Cl2 = 1:10) was used as eluent. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 8000 r.p.m. for 5 min and frozen-drying. The obtained powder was used for nanofiber fabrication.

PAN (0.4 g) and PVP (0.24 g) were completely dissolved in DMF (10 mL). Subsequently, the cobalt acetate tetrahydrate (0.7 g) was added to the above-stock solution. Then the C-dots solution was added to the solution by varying the ratio between C-dots and cobalt salt from 0 wt%, 1 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%, to 15 wt%. The mixture was stirred until a clear solution was obtained. The experimental parameters of electrospinning were adjusted as follows: the voltage was 15 kV, the working distance between the needle and collector was 17 cm, the inner diameter of the injection needle is 0.5 mm, the spinning solution perfusion rate was 0.8 mL·h−1, the ambient temperature was 25 ℃, and the ambient humidity was 30% − 40%. After the electrospinning, the as-prepared nanofibers were stripped off from the collector. The nanofibers were pre-oxidized at 250 ℃ for 2 h and further oxidized at 310 ℃ for 2 h in a Muffle furnace with a heating rate of 1 ℃/min. Then the temperature was naturally cooled down to 25 ℃, Co3O4/C-dots porous nanofibers with different C-dots contents were prepared. The Co3S4/C-dots nanofibers were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. For a typical reaction, 0.36 g Na2S·9H2O was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water to form a homogeneous solution and then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. 50 mg Co3O4/C-dots porous nanofibers were added to the solution and sealed tightly. Then the autoclave was heated in an electric oven at 160 °C for 12 h. After cooling down, the samples were taken out and washed alternately with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

Two milligrams of Co3O4/C-dots porous nanofibers were mixed with 20 mL Nafion (1%) and 180 mL ethanol, and the as-prepared mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h to obtain a well-dispersed mixture. Carbon paper was cut into 10 × 30 mm2, further cleaned by ultrasound in acetone and alcohol successively, dried in a vacuum oven at 40 ℃ for 12 h, and placed in a drying dish for further use. One side of the carbon paper was coated with epoxy glue. After solidification, the as-prepared ink was dropped on the other side of the carbon paper with an active area of 5 × 5 mm2 with a loading amount of 12.5 mg.

Regulus-8100 (Hitachi) field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) was used to obtain SEM images of the nanofibers. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and selective area electron diffraction (SAED) images were collected on JEM-2100Plus (JEOL) TEM at 200 kv to observe the microstructure of the Co3O4/C-dots porous nanofibers. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet iS5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific) spectrometer with a resolution of 1 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using Axis Supra+ (Shimadzu) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer to determine the elemental composition and characterize the element valence. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out by Ultima IV (Rigaku) X-ray diffractometer to realize phase characterization.

All electrochemical measurements applied in a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai) were carried out in a standard three-electrode system consisting of a saturated Hg/HgO reference electrode, a graphite counter electrode, and a 50 mL 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. The potential measured on the Hg/HgO reference electrode was converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode according to the Evs.RHE = Evs.Hg/HgO + 0.0592 pH + 0.098. A series of cyclic voltammetries (CVs) were carried out at sweep rates of 10 – 100 mV·s−1 at non-faradaic overpotentials to estimate the double-layer capacitance (Cdl). Electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) is directly proportional to the capacitance of the double layer (Cdl), ECSA = Cdl / Cs, where Cs is the specific capacitance of a standard electrode, and Cdl is the measured double-layer capacitance. The polarization curve reflects the change of electrode current with potential, measured by linear scanning voltammetry (LSV), and the scanning rate is selected as 10 mV·s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in potentiostatic mode at open circuit potential, applying a sinusoidal voltage with an amplitude of 5 mV and scanning frequency from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. The EIS was modeled using a simplified Randles equivalent circuit, which consists of a resistor in series (Rs) with a parallel arrangement of a resistor (Rct) and a constant phase element on Zview software. Long-term cycle stability of the as-prepared anode based on nanofibers was assessed using the chronopotentiometry (E-t curve), which records the potential overtime at a current density of 10 mA·cm−2.

The nanofibers were prepared using an electrospinning approach and the detailed production steps were shown in Experimental Section and Schematic S1. Two types of polymers were used to prepare the porous nanofibers. The microstructure of fibers was observed by the SEM. Fig. 1 a-d, Fig. S1-2 show the morphologies and size distributions of the nanofibers with different loading amounts of the C-dots. The presence of the C-dots does not lead to a significant effect on the morphologies of the nanofibers, while the diameters of the nanofibers decrease from 182.5 nm (0 wt%) to 156.2 nm (10 wt%) (Fig. 1b, d). Further increasing the C-dots concentration to 15 wt%, the obtained diameter of the nanofibers is around 173.5 nm (Figure S2). The increasing C-dots concentration can increase the conductivity of the electrospinning solution, leading to shorter energy storage time for the Taylor cone compared to the solution without C-dots, thus the cone jet ejection speed is accelerated. With further increasing the C-dots concentration, the electrospinning solution becomes viscous, leading to the increase of the nanofiber diameter [48–50]. The surface of as-obtained nanofibers is not very smooth as seen in their high magnification SEM images (Fig. 1a, c and Fig. S1b, d, f). Because of the very small size of the C-dots, we did not observe any of the C-dots in the nanofibers by the SEM.

Fig. 2a shows the FTIR spectra of C-dots, Co3O4 porous nanofibers, and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers. In the FTIR spectrum of Co3O4 porous nanofibers, the peaks at 665 cm−1 and 576 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of Co-O [51,52]. In the FTIR spectrum of C-dots, the peaks at 1160 cm−1, 1404 cm−1, 1458 cm−1, 1626 cm−1, 1662 cm−1, 1781 cm−1 are assigned to vibrations of CO, CN, CC, CO, OH, CH, respectively, and the vicinity of 3202 cm−1 and 3441 cm−1 corresponds to C-OH/NH stretching vibration. For the FTIR spectrum of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers, in addition to the prominent Co-O absorption peaks, the pronounced characteristic peaks at 1383 cm−1 and 1626 cm−1 appear, which is consistent with part of the characteristic peaks of C-dots. Similar variation was observed for the nanofibers with different concentrations of the C-dots (Figure S3). This result confirmed that C-dots were successfully incorporated into the as-prepared hybrid nanofibers.

TEM results reveal that the as-prepared nanofibers have a porous structure (Fig. 2b, d, e, and Fig. S4a). Because the viscosity of PAN is higher than that of the PVP, PAN forms a discontinuous phase in the final mixed solution, which has been reported in relevant pieces of literature [44,45,53]. During electrospinning, the mixed solution maintains the phase separation state, and a core–shell structure is formed in the obtained nanofibers, with PAN as the core and PVP as the shell [44,45,53]. In the calcination stage, PAN and PVP were removed successively with the increase in temperature, which led to the formation of the porous nanofibers. Such porous structural features can effectively increase the active sites of HER in the nanofibers. The inset HRTEM image indicates that the as-prepared Co3O4 porous nanofibers have good crystallinity (Fig. 2b). The measured d-spacing is ∼2.96 Å and ∼2.91 Å with an interplanar angle of 90°, corresponding to the {220} family of planes viewed along [001] zone axis. Fig. 2c is a typical HRTEM image of graphite carbon dots. The lattice planes are (020) and (003) (PDF#001–089-8849), which can be determined by d-spacing. In the inset HRTEM image of of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers, the lattice planes in the Co3O4 grain can be indexed by (220) and
1
¯
13
, and C-dots can be found in the porous between the large Co3O4 grains (Fig. 2d). Similar distributions of C-dots are observed in the Co3O4/15 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers (Fig. S4b). The SAED pattern also confirms the polycrystalline structure of Co3O4 in hybrid porous nanofibers (Fig. 2f, and Fig. S4c). In the HRTEM image of Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers (Fig. 2e), the lattice surfaces in the Co3S4 grains can be represented by (130) and (103) according to the d-spacing and the inter-plane Angle. The TEM image (Fig. 2e) and the SAED pattern (Fig. S4d) showed that Co3S4 was generated in the nanofibers after the reaction of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers with NaS2. These TEM results proved that C-dots were successfully integrated into the nanofibers.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of as-prepared C-dots, Co3O4, Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots and Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofiber. The XRD pattern of C-dots showed that C-dots exist as amorphous carbon. Peaks located at 19.2°, 31.5°, 37.1°, 38.8°, 45°, 55.9°, 59.6°, and 65.5° of Co3O4 porous nanofibers belong to the (111), (220), (311), (222) (400), (422), (511) and (440) crystal planes of cubic phase, respectively. All the diffraction peaks are attributed to the spinel Co3O4 phase (PDF#74–2120). Compared with that of the Co3O4 porous nanofiber, the diffraction peaks of the Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofiber only shifted 0.2°to the right, which may be caused by the tilt of sample placement during the test and the influence of C-dots. The XRD pattern of Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots fiber showed that the main product of vulcanized nanofibers was Co3S4 (PDF#02–0825). The crystal structure of the hybrid porous nanofibers has not changed significantly after the incorporation of C-dots.

The chemical states of different elements and the surface status of the as-prepared nanofibers were analyzed by XPS. Fig. 3b-d show the full survey of the XPS spectra, high-resolution C 1 s, and Co 2p XPS spectra of Co3O4 porous nanofibers and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers, respectively. In addition to the Co, O, and C peaks, Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers contain N, Ca, and Cl peaks, which indicates the presence of the C-dots in the nanofibers (Fig. 3b). The N element in Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers was completely contributed by C-dots, in XPS full spectra of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers, the proportion of Natom is 6.8%. Based on this value and N/C ratio of the C-dots, we calculate the portion of C-dots in fiber is around 10%, which is consistent with the adding amount of the C-dots in the spinning solution. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of the two as-prepared nanofibers can be divided into three peaks, and their peak positions are the same (Fig. 3c). The peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.6 eV were attributed to CC, CO, and CO bonds. As shown in Fig. 3d, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co 2p of Co3O4 porous nanofibers have two prominent peaks: the peak belonging to 2p1/2 located at 780.9 eV and the peak belonging to 2p3/2 located at 795.9 eV. Meanwhile, the spectrum can be fitted to Co3+, Co2+ and satellite peaks, among which the peak located at 781.4 eV and 780.1 eV represent Co3+ 2p1/2 and Co2+ 2p1/2, respectively, and the peaks located at 790.1 eV and 804.5 eV are two satellite peaks. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co 2p of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers was similar to those described above. However, all Co 2p peaks of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers have about 0.5 eV blue-shift, which means that the electron density of Co outer layer increases, indicating that the addition of C-dots brings more active electrons to the catalysts. In addition, the molar ratio of Co2+ compared to (Co2+ + Co3+) increases, indicating that the C-dots could reduce the Co3+ to Co2+ during the oxide formation process. These changes suggest C-dots could improve the electron transfer rate and form more Co2+ on the surface of the nanofibers compared to C-dots free nanofibers. In Figure S5, it can be seen that the addition of C-dots does not cause significant changes in the position and proportion of Co-O peak, while the increase in the proportion of CO peak and the increase in energy level, and the decrease in the proportion of CO peak and the decrease in energy level all indicate that the C-dots bring more free electrons compared to Co3O4, which helps to improve the catalytic performance of the catalyst.

The HER performance of the as-prepared electrocatalyst was evaluated in a three-electrode system using 1 M KOH electrolyte by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). As shown in Fig. 4 a, carbon paper has almost no catalytic property, while the electrocatalytic performance of the as-prepared nanofibers gradually increased with the increase of the content of C-dots in the samples. Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots exhibited dramatically enhanced performance with an overpotential of 383 mV at 10 mA·cm−2 compared with the Co3O4 (758 mV), Co3O4/1 wt% C-dots (696 mV), Co3O4/5 wt% C-dots (570 mV), and Co3O4/15 wt% C-dots (393 mV). The Tafel slopes were calculated from LSV curves to further evaluate the HER activity. As shown in Fig. 4b, with the increase of the C-dots content in as-prepared nanofibers, the slope of Tafel decreases first and then increases, but still less than that of the Co3O4 porous nanofibers (116.3 mV·dec−1). Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots displays a Tafel slope (93.4 mV·dec−1) smaller than those for Co3O4/1 wt% C-dots (111.9 mV·dec−1), Co3O4/5 wt% C-dots (100.3 mV·dec−1), and Co3O4/15 wt% C-dots (110.8 mV·dec−1). The change in the Tafel slope suggests that the number of active electrons in the hybrid nanofibers increases and the charge transfer ability is improved due to the addition of C-dots, which makes the kinetics of the HER more favourable [54,55]. The exchange current density of different catalysts can be determined based on the Tafel slope. The exchange current density values of Co3O4, Co3O4 with 1,5,10,15 wt% C-dots were 5.2 × 10−3, 8.9 × 10−3, 22.2 × 10−3, 1.42, 4.73 µA·cm−2, respectively. Obviously, the addition of carbon points makes the electrode reaction easier. The ECSA of the samples was evaluated by Cdl derived from CV tests with different scan rates. As shown in Fig. 4c and Figure S6, the obtained Cdl for Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots is 300.6 μF·cm−2, which is 25.9 times larger than that of the Co3O4, and 3.8 times larger than that of the Co3O4/15 wt% C-dots. This is because the continued increase of C-dots not only leads to a decrease in fiber fineness, but also leads to a decrease in the synergistic effect of C-dots and Co3O4 due to the intensification of agglomeration. Besides, Fig. 4d shows the Nyquist plots of the as-prepared hybrid nanofibers measured at the voltage corresponding to the 10 mA·cm−2, which can be fitted to the equivalent circuit (as in the inset) to obtain the charge transfer resistance (Rct) values. The fitted Rct values of Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots is 16.55 Ω, which is lower than those of the Co3O4 (31.15 Ω), Co3O4/1 wt% C-dots (38.55 Ω), Co3O4/5 wt% C-dots (21.2 Ω) and Co3O4/15 wt% C-dots (21.98 Ω).

In addition to efficient catalytic activity, cycling stability is a crucial indicator of catalytic performance. Therefore, each sample was tested for an extended period of 10 h through chronopotentiometry potential analysis to evaluate its catalytic stability. Fig. 4f shows the variation of samples in long-time durability. After 10-h operation, the overpotential of the Co3O4 and Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers at current density of 10 mA·cm−2 maintained good stability. Similarly, Figure S7a, b demonstrated good stability of the Co3O4/C-dots porous nanofibers electrode. The morphology of nanofibers after stability test was characterized and no significant changes were found (Figure S8). These results demonstrate that the electrocatalyst has excellent cycling stability and it is a highly desirable electrocatalyst material for HER.

In combination with the above results, the improved performances are mainly attributed to the following aspects: (1) the introduction of C-dots in Co3O4 effectively modulates the electronic properties of the catalyst, the C-dots act as good electron donors and acceptors, and the formation of a heterogeneous interface between the C-dots and Co3O4, which together give the hybrid nanofibers good electron transfer capability in HER, leading to better HER kinetics; (2) the abundant surface groups of C-dots adsorb water molecules to the surface of the electrocatalyst, effectively reducing the interfacial HER kinetics; (3) the porous structure and the reduction of nanofibers’ fineness due to the role of C-dots in the electrostatic spinning process provide large electrochemically active specific surface area; (4) the open geometry between the porous nanofibers makes it easier for the electrolyte to penetrate into the inner region of the electrode, where the porous nanofibers are highly exposed and contacted by the electrolyte, resulting in lower internal resistance and faster kinetics.

To further enhance the HER performance of Co3O4/C-dots composite, we conducted a hydrothermal reaction using NaS2 aqueous solution. As shown in Figure S9, the Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots porous nanofibers after hydrothermal reactionstill maintain a good fiber shape and the a Co3S4 structure was found after reaction (Fig. 3a).

The HER performance of the resulting materials, Co3S4 and Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers, was tested through electrochemical experiments, as shown in Fig. 5 . Compared with the Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots (383 mV), Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots exhibited obviously enhanced performance with an overpotential of 280.2 mV at 10 mA·cm−2. And the Tafel slope of Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots decreased significantly from 93.4 mV·dec−1 to 76.3 mV·dec−1. By calculating the Cdl value of the resulting materials, the Cdl of the Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots reaches 13.05 mF·cm−2, which is much larger than other composites. Fig. 5d shows the Nyquist plots of the Co3S4 and Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots at the voltage corresponding to the 10 mA·cm−2, which can be fitted according to the circuit diagram shown in the inset to obtain the charge transfer resistance (Rct) values. The fitted Rct value of Co3S4/10 wt% C-dots is 13.08 Ω, which is lower compared to that of the Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers.

At higher HER current density, the electrode material may experience dense bubble growth and its obstruction on the surface, resulting in a reduction of the catalyst's active surface area. Therefore, it is necessary to test the stability under high current density. As shown in Figure S7c and S10, the Co3O4/10 wt% C-dots nanofibers and the Co3S4/10 wt% C-Dots nanofibers catalysts still showed good stability at 50 mA/cm2.
Table 1 is a summary and comparison of some related work in recent years. It can be seen that our works have good HER performance as cobalt-based catalysts.

The HER performance of Co3S4/C-dots composites were significantly improved compared with that of the Co3O4 and Co3O4/C-dots composites. This means that post sulfidation is a feasible direction to further improve HER performance of cobalt-based composites.

In summary, we demonstrated a C-dots integrated porous cobalt chalcogenide nanofibers. The as-prepared hybrid nanofibers contain highly crystalline C-dots and cubic Co3O4/ Co3S4. Benefiting from the high conductivity of the C-dots and improved surface-active area, the Co3S4/C-dots nanofibers electrode achieved a current density of 10 mA·cm−2 at a low overpotential of 280.2 mV with a Tafel slope of 76.3 mV·dec−1 in 1 M KOH, which is much better compared to the Co3O4/C-dots. In addition, C-dots doped nanofibers boost the continuous electron transport and enhance the corrosion resistance and stability of the whole catalyst. These results indicated that the current fabricated hybrid nanofibers are suitable as efficient catalysts for HER. Considering the excellent catalytic properties of the C-dots integrated nanofibers, they may be useful for Li-ion battery, supercapacitor and CO2 reduction.

Bin Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Changchun Sun: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Chao Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhan Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Guangting Han: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Haiguang Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Zhao acknowledges the funding support from Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial Education Department.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Trends in the Exchange Current for Hydrogen Evolution
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.K. Nørskov, T. Bligaard, A. Logadottir, J.R. Kitchin, J.G. Chen, S. Pandelov, U. Stimming, Trends in the Exchange Current for Hydrogen Evolution, J Electrochem Soc 152(3) (2005).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CeO2/Co (OH) 2 hybrid electrocatalysts for efficient hydrogen and oxygen evolution reaction
J. Alloy. Compd.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.-C. Sung, G.-H. Lee, D.-W. Kim, CeO2/Co (OH) 2 hybrid electrocatalysts for efficient hydrogen and oxygen evolution reaction, Journal of Alloys and Compounds 800 (2019) 450-455.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Khalid, A.M. Honorato, H. Varela, L. Dai, Multifunctional electrocatalysts derived from conducting polymer and metal organic framework complexes, Nano Energy 45 (2018) 127-135.

Int. J. Hydrogen Energy

Kim, Evidencing enhanced oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions using In–Zn–Co ternary transition metal oxide nanostructures: a novel bifunctional electrocatalyst, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy 44(41) (2019) 23081-23090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile formation of 2D Co2P@ Co3O4 microsheets through in-situ toptactic conversion and surface corrosion: Bifunctional electrocatalysts towards overall water splitting
J. Power Sources
